data.title: Credo: Ramping Into An AI Super-Cycle (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

Credo Technology Group Holding (CRDO) is focusing on Active Electrical Cable (AEC) products that basically manage data traffic at incredible speeds (up to 1.6 Terabits per second). Their AECs are in high demand from data centers and hyperspaces that require a lot of compute power to train LLMs and run AI-driven apps (and not just AI-driven). In addition to AECs, Credo building a sophisticated platform that also offer advanced signal-processing chips, optical solutions, and memory tools to ensure data moves smoothly across even the largest supercomputer clusters of their customers.

The demand boost that CRDO has gone through over the past few quarters, and the momentum levels the stock price has managed to achieve thanks to that fundamental driver, is only gaining heat lately. From what I can see, Credo's connectivity solutions look much more reliable and energy efficient for its customers compared to anything else on the end market. In 2H 2026, Credo should get a material revenue boost for their new Blue Heron 224G AI scale-up retimer, and the second half of 2026 has already started (Credo reports for its fiscal Q3 2026 in about 2 weeks from now). I'm not expecting anything extraordinary in Q3 compared to what's already reflected in the consensus estimates, but I think management will upgrade its Q4 2026 guidance, noting extreme demand for AEC solutions in general. And it seems very likely to me that management will keep its narrative around Blue Heron positive, pointing to bullish momentum into FY2027. Also, the previous consensus beating history looks interesting - it's an indirect sign that the current consensus may be wrong again.

I think that as Credo is continuously ramping into the current AI super cycle with its top-notch AEC offerings, the business is likely to flourish in the next 2-3 years at least. And it makes the stock price relatively cheap already today.

Record-Breaking Q2 Results And What's In Store For Q3

Credo's fiscal Q2 2026 revenue amounted to ~$268 million, and it was a massive 272.1% YoY rise over Q2 2025, and it was more than 20% above Q12026. Seeking Alpha's data shows that Credo beat the consensus estimate by over 14%, although it looks like the initial consensus wasn't set too low for beating. In terms of non-GAAP EPS, we saw $0.67, which was 35%+ above the consensus - for the 5th time in a row, CRDO beat the bottom line consensus by >25%.

The above strength can be explained by Credo's profitability improvement - its adjusted gross margin amounted to 66.7%, and it's 4.1% above last year's metric, hence the difference to consensus. Despite rising so quickly, and expanding operations on different verticals, Credo had positive unlevered FCF of ~$56.2 million on a TTM basis, according to Seeking Alpha, because even the rise in CAPEX couldn't offset the CFO generation. In other words, Credo is getting extremely profitable in real time, and what it's spending on expansion seems to be leading to almost immediate accretive effect.

It's mu first research article on CRDO, and based on multiple other pieces on the stock, I know that the firm used to suffer from the lack of customer diversification. It didn't suffer in terms of financials - there was just a constant Damocles sword hanging above that made Credo an overall risky investment. But the tide on that front is finally turning with Credo signing its 5th hyperscaler recently - that customer has begun contributing initial revenue, and given the volumes of the other 4 large customers, I think that there's a chance that we'll see abnormal contribution from the 5th one already in Q3. The management has been quite bullish when it comes to the medium-term prospects, thus indirectly making my conviction stronger. Ahead of the upcoming Q3 release, the management decided to issue a preliminary upgrade a few days ago, raising the revenue range to $404-408 million from the initial revenue guidance of $335-345 million. The effective difference is at 19.4%, which is truly a lot.

One might question, "Why did they issue that upgrade - why not just wait for the earnings date and surprise the market in a positive way?"

I don't have the ultimate right answer for that question, but I think it's logical that they came out with an update, letting the Wall Street analysts adjust their views ahead of the date and not lead to excessive market volatility right after the print. It doesn't make the management team look great when they can't come up with a figure that actually predicts the business growth, and it works both ways.

What really matters when such updates take place is the market's reaction. On the surface, we see that while CRDO initially went up over 10%, its 1‑month correction still looks horrible (-17.33%, based on YCharts data):

And if we go deeper and take a look at how the analysts' consensus changed since the update, we'll see that the changes look minimal - yes, there was a jump of over 12% over the past 30 days when it comes to the Q3 revenue consensus, but the figure itself is still almost 5% below the new midpoint.

In Q2, when the revenue soared by >272%, the EPS went up by 857.14% YoY, so the operating leverage was massive, with the bottom line out-rising revenue by a factor of 3.15. I don't expect CRDO to keep the same level of operating leverage for Q3 because management initially guided for a slight GP margin contraction on a QoQ basis (from over 67% down to the range of 64% to 66%). But even at a 65% margin, we're likely to see a much more pronounced EPS growth than the revenue growth implied by the midpoint of the new guidance. In other words, I believe we might see Credo's EPS rising by 350-400% YoY. If so, the actual Q3 EPS should come in at $1.1875 vs. the current consensus of $0.94:

If Credo reaches that level of EPS for the quarter, management is likely to update the Q4 guidance as well, in turn leading to another round of bullish revisions from analysts. It's a vicious circle where one positive leads to another, especially when the environment remains in favor of this momentum.

Credo's Valuation Explained

The above dynamics, if they go according (or close) to the plan I've drawn, make CRDO look like a bargain in today's market. Why? Well, as I'm writing these lines, the stock is valued at less than 29x FY2027 earnings. Recall that Credo is about to report its fiscal Q3 2026, so its financial year is a bit shifted, and FY2027 comes earlier than for most public companies out there. So, if my assumption of $1.1875 in Q3 EPS comes true (or we see something close to that figure), and the market starts to reprice the FY2027 EPS, we should logically see CRDO's forward multiples dropping like rocks.

If Credo's FY2027 doubles versus FY2026 - and it won't be an excessively bullish assumption when we look at what CRDO's EPS figures did in 2025-2026 - we'll see $6.22 instead of the consensus $4.31. That would make CRDO trade at ~20x forward earnings amid 100% rise in earnings. It's basically a forward PEG of ~0.20x.

Assuming that Credo trades at 30x FY2027 earnings, having the EPS figure I'm targeting ($6.22, not $4.31), the stock turns out to be 49-50% undervalued in the medium term. So, I don't view CRDO as an overpriced semis stock like many other names in its niche these days.

Risks To My Bullish Views

My main thesis on Credo is based on multiple bullish assumptions that might fail to materialize in reality. Credo is still heavily reliant on just a couple of its key customers, so whenever one of them decides to leave for an alternative AEC solution, Credo's top line will drop. I see no way the management could save the situation if that happens.

I also make really bold assumptions regarding the Q3 EPS figure, extrapolating the operating leverage of Q2 with a discount. That doesn't look reliable, but it's the only way we can somehow predict what's coming for the quarter. The actual figure is definitely going to be different. If the difference turns out to be material, and the margin contraction that management guided for is heavier than I thought, both my Q3 and FY2027 EPS figures will be off by a mile. Credo might lose the reasons for having a premium valuation in that case (when the business growth is insufficient).

Conclusion

Credo looks like an ideal candidate to buy ahead of its upcoming earnings report release (set for after-market, 3/2/2026) because the recent consolidation has already shaken out "weak hands," while the business has only gotten stronger - as evidenced by management's update ahead of the print.

In my opinion, Credo Technology might add another 50% to its market capitalization over the next 12-24 months if it executes at least partially on the targets I've laid out in my article. So far, the level of execution has been great, judging by the recent past. I think the stock deserves a "Buy."

data.title: Nektar Therapeutics: Unique MOA Translates To Stellar AD Maintenance Data, Strong Buy
data.description:
Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR) is a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused on developing novel immunology therapies for autoimmune and chronic inflammatory diseases, founded in 1990. The company utilizes proprietary technology platforms, including PEGylation, to create drug candidates that modulate immune system imbalances. Its lead candidate is rezpegaldesleukin (REZPEG), a regulatory T cell stimulator in clinical studies for atopic dermatitis (AD), alopecia areata, and type 1 diabetes.

Most recently, Nektar released P2b AD maintenance data, and in this article I will deep-dive on that data, with comparison to the current SOC, market leaders and other competitors in earlier stage of the drug developments. For a one-sentence summary: the newly release maintenance data confirms the unique mechanism of action (MOA) for REZPEG is working as it is intended, making Nektar Tx a Strong Buy.

REZPEG, Treg, Atopic Dermatitis (AD)

Rezpegaldesleukin (REZPEG, formerly NKTR-358) is a first-in-class, PEGylated recombinant human interleukin-2 (IL-2) to act as a potent, selective stimulator of regulatory T cells (Tregs), which are a specialized subpopulation of CD4+ T cells that maintain immune tolerance, homeostasis, and prevent autoimmunity by suppressing excessive immune responses.

REZPEG is designed to treat autoimmune and inflammatory diseases by restoring immune system balance, specifically focusing on expansion of Tregs without significantly stimulating conventional T cells.

Atopic Dermatitis (AD), commonly known as eczema, is a chronic, non-contagious condition causing intensely itchy, dry, inflamed, and scaly skin. AD is classically viewed as a predominantly T helper type 2 (Th2)-skewed inflammatory disease.

MOAs for AD treatment

The most common MOA for AD treatment is through the use of Antagonist to inhibit hyperactive immune pathways, most notably IL4/13 inhibitors such as Dupixent from Sanofi/Regeneraon, Eybglass from Lilly. These inhibitors are specific and fast-acting to inhibit Th2 cell differentiation (via IL-4) and tissue inflammation (via IL-13), but it does not address the underlying overall hyperactivity of the immune system. In other words, if we think of hyperactive immune cells as a full classroom of problematic teenagers, IL4/13 inhibitors are like the strictest teachers that will beat these teenagers into obedience as they walk into the classroom. But then the teenagers will go back to be their crazy selves soon as the scary teacher leaves the room.

REZPEG, on the other hand, provide a long-term disease control by inducing the expansion and enhancement of Treg function. Enhanced Tregs produce anti-inflammatory cytokines like IL-10 and TGF-β, which dampen Th2 inflammation and promote immune homeostasis. These are all naturally-existing immunosuppressing pathways, and all REZPEG is doing is to enhance that. This is like playing classical music to the same full classroom of problematic teenagers, over and over again, with guidance from a brilliant music teacher (Treg): it will work slower, but overtime the "teenagers" calm themselves down so the effects can be long-lasting.

REZPEG P2b AD Induction Phase data - Not amazing

For the most part, REZPEG's induction phase data was not amazing, in comparison to Dupi (Dupixent) and others big players in AD. If looking at the 16-wk induction data, while it clearly shows that REZPEG is working, it is actually inferior to Dupi in key endpoints such as EASI-75: 29% (REZ) vs 52% (Dupi), EASI-90 9% (REZ) vs 26% (Dupi). The 24-wk induction data looks much better but only with low n (only n=30), and not enough n in the placebo group to solidify that result. Even if the 24 wk data can be replicated in a larger trial (which is a big "if"), it is only marginally better than Dupi.

Another perspective on the induction data reveals a more cautious outlook. REZPEG demonstrated only a ~30% improvement over placebo, even at the highest dose and frequency. More concerning is the apparent plateauing of EASI reduction at Week 16. This suggests a potential 'efficacy ceiling,' raising doubts about whether patients will see further deepening of response beyond the initial four-month period. However, skepticism faded as maintenance data showed REZPEG continued to deepen EASI reductions beyond the initial induction period, as the rest of the discussion will be focusing on that.

REZPEG P2b AD Maintenance Phase data - Astounding

While the induction data was underwhelming, the recently released maintenance phase results are exceptional for several reasons.

Most importantly, the maintenance regimen is successfully sustaining therapeutic gains achieved during induction. A standout highlight is that 83% of crossover patients maintained their EASI-75 response on a Q12W schedule. Requiring only one dose every three months offers patients a significant "treatment holiday" or "drug vacation" while keeping their symptoms firmly under control.

By comparison, REZPEG (Q12W) demonstrated maintenance rates for EASI-75, EASI-90, vIGA 0/1, and Pruritus NRS that were comparable to, or even numerically higher than, Dupilumab (QW/Q2W) as well as Sanofi’s second-generation asset, Amlitelimab (Q4W). Essentially, REZPEG shows the potential to outperform the current standard of care in the maintenance phase while simultaneously reducing dosing frequency by 83% to 92% compared to Dupilumab.

The durability of REZPEG is arguably its most impressive feature. In patients achieving EASI-75 during the induction phase, the response was exceptionally well-sustained throughout the maintenance period: 91% (Q4W) and 96% (Q12W) of patients stayed within one point of their maintenance baseline. Only 9% and 4% of patients, respectively, failed to be maintained. This strong maintaining capability significantly outperforms Dupilumab (DUPI), where approximately 26% of patients experience worsening symptoms even with more frequent weekly or bi-weekly dosing.

This highlights REZPEG’s unique Mechanism of Action (MOA)—the upregulation of Tregs—compared to DUPI’s cytokine blockade. Once the immune system is stabilized during induction, it appears to require only infrequent "booster" doses to maintain homeostasis. To keeping using a classroom analogy -

The Immune System: A room full of rowdy teenagers.REZPEG: Calming music.The REZPEG Effect: Once the music finally settles the students down, the melody lingers in their minds. You don't need to play the song constantly; a quick "reminder" tune is enough to keep the room quiet, as the initial impact resonates long after the music stops.

Beyond just maintenance, REZPEG induced new and deepening responses through Week 52. Of the "partial responders"—those meeting the EASI-50 crossover criteria but failing to achieve EASI-75 during the 16-week induction—51% reached EASI-75 and 33% reached EASI-90 by the end of the maintenance period. While these new response rates were slightly lower in the Q12W cohorts, the results remain exceptionally strong. These data suggest that partial responders have a 40–50% probability of achieving significant clinical benefit by continuing treatment. This underscores the unique advantage of REZPEG’s Treg-based mechanism of action: once initiated, the immune modulation appears to strengthen over time, leading to a progressively deeper therapeutic effect. Coupled with the long circulating half-life of REZPEG due to PEGylation, it explains why in the maintenance phase Q12w was sufficient to maintain and in some cases deepen the response.

The deepening response profile of REZPEG is most clearly illustrated by the transition from the induction phase to the maintenance phase. Between Week 16 and Week 52, there was a two-to-five-fold increase in the percentage of patients achieving EASI-100 (complete skin clearance).Collectively, these data provide compelling evidence that REZPEG continues to drive new and deeper clinical responses throughout the maintenance period.

REZPEG Safety

Main adverse event for REZPEG is injection site reaction (ISR), and majority (>95%) are mild to moderate. It is more of an inconvenience than actually affecting the QOL of the receiver, as almost all ISRs resolve quickly in a few days. Notably, no increased risk of conjunctivitis, oral ulcers, asthma, infections or MACE were observed with REZPEG. REZPEG’s safety profile stands in contrast to IL-4/13 blockers (such as Dupixent), which are associated with known side effects like conjunctivitis, oral ulcers, and increased infection rates. By utilizing a unique and differentiated Mechanism of Action (MOA), REZPEG offers a potentially superior safety alternative for patients.

Risks for NKTR Tx

The primary risk factor is the potential failure of the P3 AD trial—scheduled for Q2 2026—to replicate the strong P2b results. However, the P3 trial design incorporates several optimizations that may actually yield results superior to P2b, due to:

  1. Extended Induction Period:The P3 study will utilize a 24-week induction period, an increase from the 16-week period in P2b. Given that P2b data demonstrated increased efficacy over time, a longer induction is expected to maximize therapeutic response.
  2. Stringent Maintenance Crossover Criteria:The criteria for transitioning from the induction phase to the maintenance phase have been tightened from EASI 50 to EASI 75. By selecting a population of "stronger responders" for the maintenance phase, the likelihood of sustained or improved long-term efficacy is significantly increased.

A secondary risk involves the placebo effect during the maintenance phase. Because the P2b trial lacked a dedicated placebo arm in the maintenance phase (to preserve blinding and study objectivity), there is no direct baseline for comparison. Despite this lack of historical data, the magnitude of response observed in the P2b treatment arms was substantial. Assuming these results translate to P3, it is highly probable that a statistically significant "delta" (therapeutic gap) will be maintained between the treatment and placebo arms.

Beyond clinical trial outcomes, REZPEG faces potential commercial hurdles within the highly competitive AD market. While its unique Treg-mediated mechanism of action and potentially durable dosing schedule offer clear differentiation from established IL-4/13 inhibitors like Dupixent and Ebglyss, this novelty may initially drive provider hesitancy compared to the well-understood pathways of current biologics. Furthermore, by REZPEG’s projected 2029 launch, Dupixent will benefit from over a decade of real-world safety data and established prescriber loyalty. Nektar Therapeutics also faces a substantial resource gap; competing against the massive commercial infrastructure and marketing budgets of industry giants like Sanofi and Eli Lilly—who are incentivized to aggressively defend their top-selling assets—presents a formidable challenge for a smaller biotech firm attempting to capture market share.

Financial health and recent offering

As of last week, Nektar Therapeutics has strengthened its financial position through significant capital raises—including a $460 million public offering recently—providing a cash runway to support operations and clinical trials into 2027 and potentially beyond. While this offering totaled 7,637,931 shares of common stock at $58.00 per share and represented significant, roughly 30-35% dilution for existing shareholders, it is a welcomed news for investors. In fact, on the day of offering announcement NKTR's stock was up 18.5%, one day after its AD maintenance data release date when stock shot up 51%. This indicates a strong appetite for its shares and upbeat sentiment surrounding its recent release of AD maintenance data as well as the future prospect of NKTR.

Despite previous downsizing and operating losses after termination of collaboration with Lilly and BMS, the company is finally looking like it is turning around with strong execution and hitting strikes with great clinical data like discussed in this article.

Valuation for Nektar Tx

There are a few ways to valuate Nektar Tx. First is by projected revenue. Dupixent is projected to remain the dominant therapy in atopic dermatitis (AD) toward 2030, with total, multi-indication sales for the drug expected to exceed 20 billion by that year. Along with other players, AD is likely a 40-50B+ market by 2035, according to Apogee Tx' estimate. If REZPEG P3 data looks as amazing as P2 (assigning 80% chance), then its chance of approval will also likely be very high (assigning 85%). Assuming 15% market penetration peak sale, which can be conservative given the excellent data we are seeing so far:

Peak revenue REZPEG in AD by 2035 = 50B x 15% x 80% x 85% = 5.1B

REZPEG is also looking promising in other indications like Alopecia Areata (AA), which can be another 1B opportunity, assuming 50% of success.

AA by 2035 = 1B X 50% = 0.5 B

So if total revenue 2035 = 5.6B, and using a conservative PS ratio of 3 for pharma industry, by then NKTR will be valued at

5.6B x 3 = 16.8 B,

which is ~8.4 X its current market cap of 2B. I expect that NKTR's valuation will rise drastically to that over time, even in the short term as its maintenance data is fully digested by the market. P3 AD topline readout will be the key inflection point to close the gap between current 2B and 10B+.

Focusing on short term valuations, we can look at NKTR's close peer Apogee therapeutics (APGE), which owns Zumilokibart (APG777). It is the company's lead asset which is a subcutaneous monoclonal antibody targeting IL-13. It is currently in P2 for AD, and they have induction data and are waiting for maintenance data, so timeline-wise the two companies are very close. For the induction data comparison (see table below), APG777 induction EASI75 Delta was better than REZPEG's, 42% vs 29%, however they are similar on other endpoints such as EASI90, IGA 0/1 and Itch NRS delta.

Drug Name (Company) Mechanism of action Induction EASI75 Delta Induction EASI90 Delta

Induction

Dupixent (SNY/REGN) IL4/13 37% (P3) 25.8% (P3) 27.8% (P3) 18B revenue 2025

APG777 employs the same MOA as Dupi, so it is hard to imagine its maintenance data will be as good as REZPEG's. Currently Apogee Therapeutics has a 4.57B market cap, which is 125% higher than Nektar Tx at last check. As such, Nektar remains undervalued both from future revenue perspective and by comparing to peer's valuation, and is therefore rated Strong buy.

data.title: Data Centres And Debt: How The AI Building Boom Is Reshaping Credit Markets
data.description:
As AI companies raise billions of dollars to finance AI infrastructure projects, corporate bond supply is surging. Rachana Bhat, Vice President and Director, Investment Grade Credit Portfolio Management, TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to discuss the long-term growth and potential refinancing risks of AI’s building boom.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: The AI hyperscalers have unveiled billions of spending this year, with some companies doubling the amount they plan to spend on development compared to last year. That news hasn't been taken all that well by the equity market this round. How is the bond market viewing all this? Joining us now to discuss is Rachana Bhat, VP and director of Investment-Grade Credit Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management.

Rachana, always great to have you here with us.

Rachana Bhat: Likewise. Great to be here. And Happy Friday.

Greg Bonnell: Happy Friday, indeed. AI keeps being the big story. The hyperscalers had been promising, heading to this year, big spending. We just came out of an earnings period. What did they actually tell us about their spending plans?

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, Greg, so what we've learned is, the spending is not slowing-- it's accelerating. So when you look at the big hyperscalers, which is Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), i.e., Google-- there's Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), and Oracle (ORCL). They are spending-- or they came into the year expecting that they'll spend more than $500 billion combined. But that number was revised to more than $650 billion. So that's up about 65% since last year's spending.

Now, most of that upside surprise in capex has actually come from Amazon and Google-- so thankfully, companies that have very, very solid balance sheets. Now, that's for the year. Now, when you think about, call it, the next five years, the spending expectation is for $5 trillion. So let's say, on an average, $1 trillion combined for those five hyperscalers.

Let me contextualize this for you. If you take S&P 500 in 2024, added all the capex for the companies back then, the number would have been $950 billion-- so less than what these five companies are spending just by themselves on an average over the next five years.

So yes, this is very large, concentrated investment cycle. And that's what makes it very important as well. These companies are spending to build out the technical infrastructure to support AI and cloud demand. So a lot of these companies, you'll see, will shift to negative net free cash flow-- which is free cash flow minus capex minus shareholder returns. What happens when your free cash flow is negative? Well, you need to borrow more money. So they'll be issuing a lot more debt as they fund this buildout over the coming years.

Greg Bonnell: Now, last year, maybe some exceptions, but every time one of the big hyperscalers came and said, man, we're going to spend so much money, the market said, we love it. They rewarded the share price. This latest round, though, these big numbers that came out-- and we were warned they'd be big numbers-- the stocks would pull back on that news. So that's the equity side. What about the bond side? Are bond sides concerned about the fundamentals here, the bond investors?

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, so what was interesting for me, even on the equity side is, if you thought about this a couple of months ago, numbers like this alongside the earnings beats that we've seen was driving a lot of enthusiasm. Now, the same numbers are being seen from a negative lens now. And that's because the investors are now focused a lot more on return on investment, the path to monetization. And rightfully so-- because this capex is accelerating, and it's rising from very elevated levels.

Now, when you shift to the credit side, or the bond side, if you look at-- things have been calm as compared to equities. When you look at the tech-- so IG tech sector spreads, they're actually flat on the year, very much in line with what the broader market has done. So the IG tech sector spreads over this year have actually been flat.

The big reason for that is these companies-- with the exception of Oracle-- are sitting on tons of cash, a lot of free cash flow. Yes, I said that free cash flow will be going negative as we move on over the next few years, but the idea is that these companies have a lot of room on their balance sheets before that downgrade happens. And even if that downgrade happens, well, guess what? They will still be in a very high-quality bucket.

I'll give you an example. So, S&P recently affirmed Google's ratings at AA plus. They said, well, guess what-- Google can come and issue 180 billion in net incremental debt before there's a downgrade concern. For context, Google this year is expected to issue about $30-35 billion, which, for the most part, it's already done. So technically, it means Google can come five more times, given that $180 billion number--

Greg Bonnell: Not get anyone nervous.

Rachana Bhat: Exactly, before that downgrade happens. And guess what? Even if that downgrade happens, you're just going from AA plus to AA mid. For these companies to be downgraded to BBB-- again, with the exception of Oracle-- Google will have to drive truckloads of debt before that even happens.

So, very different story than what you have seen within equities. And because of that strong fundamental, the balance sheet, the ratings buffer these guys have, the strong balance sheets they have, IG credit as an asset class can actually withstand a lot more in terms of, call it, the AI disruption or monetization challenges and be more resilient-- which is why you've seen tech IG sector spreads hold up pretty well.

Greg Bonnell: So those are big numbers in terms of issuance, just for Google alone. Of course, there's several of them that are going to be issuing debt. And some of it's a hundred-year debt. Hundred-year bonds. Is there demand out there to meet this issuance?

Rachana Bhat: You've done your homework. So, oh my God, tons of demand. Tons of demand. So, if anything, the problem is quite the opposite, which is they're coming out with so much supply of bonds, but we as investors are not getting enough.

Take Google. So Google came to market and said, hey, I want to raise $15 billion in debt in the US investment-grade market. The demand? $100 billion. So they upsized to $20 billion. They took $20 billion in the proceeds from this debt-raising cycle. Well, it was easy for them, so it makes sense. Great job, right, from Google's perspective.

Now, you pointed to the hundred-year bond. So what's really interesting for Google is they actually also went to the sterling market to issue about-- call it more than 5 billion in debt. And one of the tranches there for what they issued was a hundred-year bond. Very, very, very rare for corporates. It's usually mostly reserved for governments, high-quality governments and, call it, long-established universities. For example, Oxford University has a hundred-year bond.

Greg Bonnell: What's going to be around in a hundred years is the question, right?

Rachana Bhat: So Oxford University has been there since 1300s, and it's structurally important for the country. So it makes sense. But Google, that's a tech company issuing a hundred-year bond. So to me, from my perspective, this is a very bullish signal, if you will, from Google's perspective, because they're issuing at very high interest rates. So they're showing you confidence in their AI journey that they will be able to get this money back. And the demand for that hundred-year bond, by the way, that tranche was greater than nine times oversubscribed. So, pretty impressive there.

You know, people can still have concerns around, oh, can these companies still come to the market, and are there funding concerns? We've heard a lot around that. I would tell them to look at Oracle. So Oracle, as an example, is everything what market was concerned about-- high leverage, but leverage still rising up. And then you have OpenAI concentration, uncertainty around OpenAI being able to pay them in the future, and lots of debt that was at risk of falling into high yield from investment-grade. So, many bad things. Look what happens.

So Oracle comes to market wanting to issue $25 billion in debt. The demand? $130 billion. So really, no demand concerns out there, especially given, if you think about these companies-- again, outside of Oracle-- they have choice whether they want to come and issue in this market because they have lots of cash and free cash flow. So they can say, well, the market is not behaving well. I'm going to step back. I don't need to issue. So that optionality really helps.

And to me, there are three things that are really supporting that demand. So the first one is-- if you could bring up the chart now-- the first one is, if you look at the tech investment-grade sector spreads, look at that arrow. It's already jumped up. And for clarity, spreads are the extra compensation you earn when you buy a corporate bond over a government bond. So that compensation has risen, as you can see. So it's already adjusted for this supply expectation, if you will.

The second reason is we as investors-- this has been very well-publicized and written about-- so we as investors were positioned for this supply.

The third is balance sheet. As we discussed, the fundamentals are in pretty good shape. And where they are not-- which is in the case of Oracle-- we as investors are getting well-compensated for it. So yes, the demand story is quite supportive from an investment-grade lens.

Greg Bonnell: Now, if you go back to the pullback we saw on some of the equity side of the hyperscalers on these plans, I mean, there's 2-3% on any given day. The sharper selloff has been in the software stocks because of all the fear that AI is going to come in and just disrupt their businesses to the point where people will use the AI and not them. So a lot of risk, fear there on the equity side. Is any of that showing up in the broader credit market?

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, so what's been fascinating for me, Greg, is that when you look at the equity market, it's not just the disruptors, so guys who are actually building or helping build the AI infrastructure, but also the disruptees, which are the software services companies which will be at risk if this buildup is successful. The stocks were getting hit at the same time. So that was very fascinating for me.

Now, when you look at this from a credit lens, there are real concerns. But those concerns, from a credit standpoint, are not evenly distributed. What I mean by that is that pressure is really concentrated within low-quality, smaller companies that have very high leverage. Why? Because high leverage, they need to pay debt. You have these companies that are being challenged. Their business models are being challenged. Their stock valuations are falling. If stock valuations are falling, that means their underlying asset values are falling. And in that case, your credit metrics are weakening a lot more, and very quickly. So this could actually take these companies towards default as well. So, it's very important to be careful of those pockets.

Now, those pockets are actually concentrated within leveraged loan and private credit. But they are lower-quality pockets in those, call it, asset classes-- about 15% to 20% exposure within leveraged loans and private credit to these smaller companies that are highly leveraged. In high yield, that exposure is less than 5%.

What's important to watch here, in my view, is the refinancing activity. So there's refinancing wall coming in 2028, which is a big wall. How and when they tackle those will be important to watch, especially with all of what's been going on.

But one of the things that I think about right now is, the lower you go in quality, the more dispersion there is within fundamentals and valuation. So, it's extremely, extremely important to be selective. For example, if you look at high yield on this, it has less than 5% exposure. So it is pretty insulated relative to, call it, leveraged loans and private credit.

High yield, on the surface, has not really widened much for the year. But when you look at high-yield tech sector, which is where some of these smaller software companies are concentrated, high-yield sector spreads or high-yield tech sector spreads have widened by a hundred basis points.

So selectivity really matters. You can't just close your eyes and buy the index. It is a stock picker's market. It is a bond picker's market. And having, call it, experienced managers manage it for you, that have the resources, big teams that are looking through financials, cash flows, covenants, coverage is extremely important.

Now, when you look at investment-grade-- let's shift to investment-grade a little bit here-- the problem credits are far and few because we don't have small companies within investment-grade. Most of the exposure within investment-grade comes indirectly through BDC exposure. But that BDC exposure is less than 1% of the IG market. So, quite insulated on that front.

So when I think about the broader credit space, to me, investment-grade really provides that resilience and has a strong role to play in portfolios today, because I can earn that steady, relatively steady income without all the worry that's going on in the market because of all the solid fundamentals that we discussed.
